short.headline: How long do Indians live?
short.dek: Life expectancy at birth has nearly doubled since 1960, but healthy years lag behind.
short.body: An Indian born today can expect to live about 72 years, a huge jump from 46 years in 1960. But those extra years are not all healthy: at birth, a person can expect only 58 years in full health. That gap of roughly 14 years means many Indians live their final years with disease or disability. The gains so far have come mainly from saving children, under-five mortality has fallen from over 200 per 1,000 births to 28. Now the challenge is to extend healthy years at older ages. Women live longer than men: 75 years vs 72, but the gender gap in healthy years is small.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this actually mean, macha?
macha.body: Basically, we are living much longer than our grandparents did. A baby born today can expect to see their 72nd birthday, that's 26 years more than someone born in 1960. But here's the catch: about 14 of those years are likely to be spent with some illness or disability. So we are living longer, but not necessarily healthier. The big reason we got here is that fewer children are dying young, that pulled the average up. Now the real task is to make sure the extra years we gain are good years, not just extra years of sickness. That means spending more on healthcare, right now India spends just 3.3% of its GDP on health, which is low given the needs.
macha.soWhat: Indians are living longer, but the next frontier is living better, healthier old age needs more investment.

article.title: How long do Indians live?
article.standfirst: Life expectancy has risen sharply, but the quality of those extra years remains a challenge.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## How long does the average Indian live today?

An Indian born in 2024 can expect to live about 72.24 years, according to the World Bank. That's a remarkable jump from 1960, when life expectancy was just 45.61 years, a gain of nearly 27 years in six decades. The United Nations projects that by 2030, the average will rise to 73.63 years. Women consistently outlive men: by 2030, the UN expects women to live 75.41 years and men 71.99 years. But these are averages, they hide big differences between rich and poor, and between states.

## How does India compare with other countries?

India's life expectancy of 72.24 years in 2024 is slightly below the world average of 73.48 years. China is at 78.02 years, and the United States at 78.89 years. But India has been catching up: in 1960, India was at 45.61 years, far behind the world average of 50.94 years. The gap has narrowed from over 5 years to just over 1 year. The progress is real, but India still lags peers.

## Are those years healthy years?

Not entirely. The World Health Organization estimates that in 2021, an Indian at birth could expect only 58.15 years in full health, that's about 14 years less than the total life expectancy. This 'healthy life expectancy' includes years lived without disease or disability. So while Indians are living longer, many of those extra years are spent with illness. The gap between total and healthy life expectancy is a measure of the burden of disease, it means the healthcare system keeps people alive but not always well.

## What about people who reach 60?

An Indian who turns 60 in 2021 can expect to live another 15.62 years on average, according to WHO. Women at 60 have 16.51 years left, men 14.76 years. That's fewer years than in many richer countries, but the number has been slowly rising. Life expectancy at 60 is important because it tells us about health in old age, and as more Indians survive childhood, improving old-age health becomes the next big challenge.

## How much has life expectancy changed since 1960?

Dramatically. From 45.61 years in 1960 to 72.24 years in 2024, that's a gain of over 26 years. The biggest gains happened in the 1960s-80s, when child mortality fell sharply. In recent decades, the pace has slowed, but the upward trend continues. The UN projects that by 2030, life expectancy will reach 73.63 years. The long view is clear: India has achieved a quiet revolution in living longer.

## What has driven this increase in life expectancy?

The single biggest driver is the sharp drop in child deaths. In 2023, the under-five mortality rate was 27.75 per 1,000 live births, down from over 200 per 1,000 in 1960. That means far fewer children are dying before age five. Because life expectancy is an average, saving a child who would have died adds many decades to the average. The infant mortality rate (deaths in the first year) was 24.24 per 1,000 live births in 2023, also down sharply. These declines reflect better healthcare, immunisation, and nutrition.

## How does health spending relate to life expectancy?

In 2023, India spent just 3.3% of its GDP on current health expenditure, down from 4.1% in 2000. That is low compared to most countries. The fact that life expectancy has continued to rise despite relatively low health spending suggests that other factors, like sanitation, education, and economic growth, have also played a role. But the gap between total and healthy life expectancy (about 14 years) hints that more spending on healthcare could improve the quality of those extra years.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: A data-driven look at how long Indians live, how that has changed over time, and whether those years are healthy.

pullQuotes.quote: An Indian born in 2024 can expect to live 72.24 years, nearly 27 years more than someone born in 1960.
But only 58.15 of those years are in full health, meaning about 14 years are lived with disease or disability.
pullQuotes.numberLabel: Healthy life expectancy at birth (WHOSIS_000002): NumericValue (2021)

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of life expectancy as the average age a newborn baby can expect to reach if current death rates stay the same. It's not a prediction for any one person, it's a statistical average for a whole group. If you hear 'life expectancy at birth is 72 years', it means that if the death rates of today hold, a baby born today would live to about 72. It does not mean everyone dies at 72, many live longer, some die younger. And it does not account for future medical advances or crises.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Life expectancy is the single best summary of a country's health. It captures both child survival and old-age mortality. On this page, we use it to answer 'How long do Indians live?' and to track India's progress over time.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: This is like life expectancy but only counts years lived in good health, without disease, disability, or serious illness. Imagine two people both live to 70, but one is healthy all the way and the other spends their last 10 years with chronic illness. Healthy life expectancy would be 70 for the first and 60 for the second. It's a measure of the quality of life, not just length.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India's total life expectancy is 72 years, but healthy life expectancy is only 58 years. That gap tells us people are living longer but not necessarily better. It points to the burden of non-communicable diseases like diabetes and heart disease.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: A mortality rate is the number of deaths in a specific group over a specific time, usually per 1,000 people or per 1,000 live births. For example, 'under-five mortality rate of 27.75 per 1,000 live births' means that out of every 1,000 babies born, about 28 die before their fifth birthday. It's a way to compare death risk across different populations or time periods.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Child mortality rates are the biggest drivers of life expectancy. When fewer children die, the average life expectancy shoots up. On this page, we look at under-five and infant mortality to explain why Indian life expectancy has increased so much.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: This is the standard denominator for child mortality. Instead of saying '27.75 out of every 1,000 babies die before age five', we say '27.75 per 1,000 live births'. It allows easy comparison across countries and years, regardless of population size.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: All the child mortality numbers on this page use this unit. It helps you compare India's progress to other countries or to its own past.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: When comparing death rates across populations with different age structures (e.g., one country has many young people, another many old), statisticians adjust the numbers to what they would be if both groups had the same age distribution. This is 'age-standardization'. It removes the effect of age so you can compare the 'real' health risk.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Life expectancy is already age-standardized in a sense, it's calculated from current age-specific death rates. But when comparing different groups, age standardization is often used to avoid misleading conclusions. Not used directly in the charts here, but it's the reason life expectancy comparisons are fair.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: Life expectancy by sex at birth
visualizationDecisions.reason: Shows trend and gender gap clearly on a phone line chart.
Multiple lines for India, China, US, World, line chart best for comparison.
Single line showing trend over time.
Single line to emphasize the long-term rise.

visualizationDecisions.indicatorOrVisual: Health expenditure as a share of GDP
visualizationDecisions.reason: Shows the decline in spending share.

chartExplainers.title: Life expectancy at birth, by sex
chartExplainers.takeaway: By 2030, Indian women are projected to live 75.41 years, men 71.99 years.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows three lines: overall, male, and female life expectancy from 2000 to 2030. The UN data includes estimates up to 2024 and projections beyond. In 2000, overall life expectancy was 62.75 years. By 2030, it rises to 73.63 years. The gap between men and women remains steady at about 3.5 years. Women benefit from biological advantages and lower mortality at most ages. The projections assume current trends continue, they are not guarantees.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It directly answers the page question with the most authoritative source, showing both the current level and future trajectory.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Read the lines from left (2000) to right (2030). The vertical axis shows years, horizontal shows years.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse the median projection with a prediction. It is a 'most likely' scenario, not a certainty.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use thick lines with clear color coding (blue for male, pink for female, green for both).

chartExplainers.title: India vs the world
chartExplainers.takeaway: India’s life expectancy (72.24 years in 2024) is below China (78.02) and the US (78.89), but above the world average (73.48).
chartExplainers.detail: This chart compares India, China, the United States, and the world average from 1960 to the latest year. In 1960, India was at 45.61 years, far behind the world average of 50.94. By 2024, India has nearly caught up to the world average. China has surged ahead, it was at 33.42 in 1960 (lower than India) but now leads. The US line is flat or slightly declining in recent years.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Gives context, how India is doing relative to peers and the world.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each line is a country or the world. The higher the line, the longer people live.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume that India is 'catching up' to China in a linear way, China has grown faster.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Limit to four lines with clear labels.

chartExplainers.title: Healthy life expectancy at birth
chartExplainers.takeaway: In 2021, Indians could expect only 58.15 years in full health.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows healthy life expectancy (HALE) over time. In 2021, the overall HALE was 58.15 years, about 14 years less than total life expectancy. This gap means that on average, an Indian spends their last 14 years with some disease or disability. The pandemic may have affected recent data. The trend shows a slow increase, but not as fast as total life expectancy.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It qualifies the headline number, living longer is not the same as living better.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line shows average years in good health at birth.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not think HALE is the same as life expectancy. It is always lower.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Single line with clear annotation of the gap with total life expectancy.

chartExplainers.title: Remaining life expectancy at age 60
chartExplainers.takeaway: An Indian who turns 60 in 2021 can expect to live another 15.62 years.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows how many more years a person aged 60 can expect to live. In 2021, it was 15.62 years for both sexes combined. Women have 16.51 years, men 14.76 years. This number has been rising slowly. It is a key measure for pension systems and healthcare planning for the elderly.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It shifts focus from children to older adults, the next frontier for extending life.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Read the value for the latest year: e.g., 15.62 years at age 60.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse this with life expectancy at birth, it is calculated from age 60 onward.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a line chart with a clear latest value label.

chartExplainers.title: Life expectancy since 1960
chartExplainers.takeaway: Indian life expectancy has nearly doubled from 45.61 years in 1960 to 72.24 years in 2024.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the remarkable long-term trend. The line rises steeply from 1960 to about 2000, then continues upward but more slowly. The gains are driven by falling child mortality, better nutrition, and improved healthcare. The overall increase of 26.63 years in 64 years is one of the biggest gains in the world.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It provides the dramatic historical perspective, how far India has come.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line moves from left (1960) to right (2024). The higher the line, the longer people live.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume the same rate of increase will continue, it naturally slows as baseline rises.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Annotate start and end values directly on the line.

chartExplainers.title: Under-five child mortality rate
chartExplainers.takeaway: In 2023, 27.75 out of every 1,000 Indian children died before age five, down from over 200 in 1960.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows the dramatic decline in under-five mortality. The rate has fallen from very high levels to 27.75 per 1,000 live births in 2023. That means the chance of a child surviving to age five has improved enormously. Since child deaths pull down life expectancy, this decline is the main reason life expectancy has risen so much.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It explains the main driver behind rising life expectancy.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line shows deaths per 1,000 live births over time. Lower is better.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not confuse under-five mortality with infant mortality, infant is only first year.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use a log scale or strong annotation of the drop.

chartExplainers.title: Infant mortality rate
chartExplainers.takeaway: In 2023, 24.24 out of 1,000 infants died before their first birthday.
chartExplainers.detail: Infant mortality, deaths in the first year of life, was 24.24 per 1,000 live births in 2023. This is slightly lower than the under-five rate because most child deaths occur in infancy. The rate has been falling steadily, reflecting improvements in neonatal care, vaccination, and maternal health.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It focuses on the most vulnerable period, the first year, and shows the impact of healthcare access.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line shows deaths per 1,000 live births under age one.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not think infant mortality includes stillbirths, it does not.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Keep the scale consistent with the under-five chart for easy comparison.

chartExplainers.title: Health expenditure as a share of GDP
chartExplainers.takeaway: India spent just 3.3% of its GDP on health in 2023, down from 4.1% in 2000.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows current health expenditure as a percentage of GDP. The share has declined from 4.1% in 2000 to 3.3% in 2023. That is a low level even by developing country standards. It suggests that the gains in life expectancy have come despite relatively low public health spending, probably due to other factors like economic growth and sanitation improvements.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It provides structural context, how much the country invests in health.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The line shows percentage of GDP spent on health. Higher means more resources.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume that higher spending automatically means better health, efficiency matters too.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Annotate the decline with a clear callout.

sourceNotes: UN World Population Prospects 2024 for life expectancy by sex and projections.
World Bank (SP.DYN.LE00.IN) for India life expectancy since 1960 and comparison data.
WHO Global Health Observatory (WHOSIS_000002) for healthy life expectancy.
WHO GHO (WHOSIS_000015) for life expectancy at age 60.
WHO GHO (MDG_0000000007) for under-five mortality rate.
WHO GHO (MDG_0000000001) for infant mortality rate.
WHO GHO (GHED_CHEGDP_SHA2011) for current health expenditure as share of GDP.
All India data is national. Subnational breakdowns not included.
caveats: Life expectancy is a period measure based on current death rates, not a cohort forecast for any individual.
UN projections are median scenarios and may differ from actual outcomes.
Healthy life expectancy estimates have uncertainty ranges; the gap with total life expectancy is approximate.
Child mortality estimates are from UN IGME and may differ from India's Sample Registration System (SRS) official data.
Health expenditure data does not include out-of-pocket spending or private sector efficiency.

plannedCharts.title: Life expectancy at birth, by sex
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single clearest number for how long Indians live. Three lines, male, female, both sexes, from 2000 to 2030, with the UN's median projection visible after 2024.

plannedCharts.title: India vs the world
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · 1960 to latest · life expectancy at birth
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's life expectancy in the context of China, the United States, and the world average. The convergence and the gap are both visible.

plannedCharts.title: Healthy life expectancy at birth
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Years lived are one thing. Years lived in full health, without disease or disability, is what people actually experience.

plannedCharts.title: Remaining life expectancy at age 60
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Life expectancy at birth is dominated by child mortality. Survival at 60 measures health in later life, where the next gains must come from.

plannedCharts.title: Life expectancy since 1960
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Zoomed out to six decades, the transformation is stark: Indian life expectancy has nearly doubled, from 46 to 72 years.

plannedCharts.title: Under-five child mortality rate
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Falling child deaths are the main engine that pushed Indian life expectancy from 46 to 72. Every child who survives to five adds a full lifetime.

plannedCharts.title: Infant mortality rate
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The first year of life is the single riskiest. Infant deaths are the most dependent on institutional healthcare, skilled birth attendants, neonatal units, immunisation.

plannedCharts.title: Health expenditure as a share of GDP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Life expectancy is not just biology. It is also a function of what a country spends on health. This provides that structural context.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
